1: τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ τρίτῃ — Counting from the "next day" of 1:43, this is the third day of that sequence, giving the opening week of Jesus's ministry a structured seven-day shape in the Evangelist's narration. The phrase carries no explicit resurrection symbolism here, but the Evangelist is rarely casual about numerology. Gamos (G1062) is the wedding feast, not merely the ceremony; Kanā tēs Galilaias is specified to distinguish it from a Cana in the Phoenician region.
2: ἐκλήθη δὲ καὶ ὁ Ἰησοῦς — The passive eklēthē (was invited) places Jesus in the position of a guest, not the host. The inclusion of his disciples suggests that by this point Jesus was understood to travel with a retinue — the social convention of a teacher and his students dining together as a group was recognized and accommodated.
3: ὑστερήσαντος οἴνου — The aorist participle hysterēsantos (having fallen short/run out) indicates the wine supply is already exhausted when Mary speaks. Her statement οἶνον οὐκ ἔχουσιν (they have no wine) is factual but functions as an implicit appeal — she reports the situation to one she expects can act. The Evangelist records no explicit request.
4:
τί ἐμοὶ καὶ σοί, γύναι — Gynai (G1135, Woman) is a vocative used by Jesus also at 19:26 (to Mary at the cross) and 20:15 (to Mary Magdalene); it is not dismissive in Greek convention but a respectful address. The idiom ti emoi kai soi — "what to me and to you" — appears in OT contexts (Judg 11:12; 2 Sam 16:10; 1 Kgs 17:18) as a distancing formula marking that two parties have different concerns about a situation, not hostility.

οὔπω ἥκει ἡ ὥρα μου — Hōra (G5610, hour) is theologically loaded in the Fourth Gospel: it refers to the appointed moment of Jesus's glorification through death (7:30; 8:20; 12:23; 13:1; 17:1). "My hour has not yet come" signals that the wedding miracle is not the decisive hour; that moment will come in chs. 12–19. The "not yet" (oupō) implies it is coming.
5: ὅ τι ἂν λέγῃ ὑμῖν ποιήσατε — Mary's instruction to the servants (diakonois) uses an aorist imperative poiēsate with an indefinite relative clause (hoti an legē — whatever he says): unconditional and comprehensive obedience is called for. Her response to the rebuff in v.4 is neither resentment nor collapse; she proceeds in confidence.
6: λίθιναι ὑδρίαι ἕξ — Stone (lithinos, G3035) water jars are specified because stone vessels could not contract ritual impurity under Levitical law (Lev 11:33 governs clay vessels; stone is exempt). Jewish households that observed toharāh (purity) law kept stone vessels for precisely this reason. Six jars — not seven (the complete number) — may indicate an incomplete state, though the Evangelist does not press this point. The quantity (20–30 gallons each; the Greek metrētas duo ē treis) gives a total of roughly 120–180 gallons: a striking excess of provision.
7: γεμίσατε τὰς ὑδρίας ὕδατος — The command is to fill the jars with water, not wine: the servants act in ordinary faith with ordinary water. Heōs anō — to the brim, to the top — signals their complete obedience. No gap or reservation is left for adding anything else; the transformation, when it occurs, cannot be attributed to an adulteration of the water.
8: ἀρχιτρικλίνῳ — The compound architriklinos (G755) appears nowhere else in the Bible and rarely in classical literature. It combines archi- (chief/head) and triklinos (three-couch dining room, the standard formal dining arrangement). The role was roughly that of a master of ceremonies, chosen from the guests, who regulated the dinner, tasted the wine, and managed the pace of drinking. He would have professional expertise in judging wine quality.
9: ὡς δὲ ἐγεύσατο ὁ ἀρχιτρίκλινος τὸ ὕδωρ οἶνον γεγενημένον — The perfect participle gegenēmenon (having become wine) marks a transformation already completed by the time of tasting: the water has become wine and remains wine. At what moment the transformation occurred the text does not say; the Evangelist locates it between the filling and the drawing-out, keeping attention on the result rather than the process.
10: πᾶς ἄνθρωπος πρῶτον τὸν καλὸν οἶνον τίθησιν — The master of the feast states a universal social convention: good wine (kalon oinon) comes first, inferior wine (elassō, the lesser) comes when guests are less discerning. Methysthōsin (when they have become drunk, G3184) is the word used without euphemism in the Synoptics and Pauline letters for drunkenness; the master of the feast describes normal banquet practice. Tetērēkas (you have kept, perfect): the good wine has been kept and kept available — a permanent state of provision.
11: Ταύτην ἐποίησεν ἀρχὴν τῶν σημείων ὁ Ἰησοῦς — The Evangelist marks this formally as the archēn tōn sēmeiōn (beginning of the signs). Sēmeion (G4592, sign) is John's consistent vocabulary for Jesus's miracles: he never uses teras (wonder/portent) or dynamis (power/mighty work), the Synoptic terms. A sign points beyond itself to what it signifies — in this case, the glory (doxa, G1391) of Jesus. The disciples believed (episteusan) as a result: faith follows the revelation of glory.
12: κατέβη εἰς Καφαρναούμ — The verb katebē (went down) is geographically accurate: Cana sits in the hills of Galilee while Capernaum is on the shore of the Sea of Galilee, at a lower elevation. The mention of hoi adelphoi autou (his brothers) alongside his mother and disciples is a matter-of-fact family reference; the relationship of Jesus's brothers to the discipleship community will become complex later (7:5).
13: ἤγγιζεν τὸ πάσχα τῶν Ἰουδαίων — Pascha (G3957, Passover) is the first of three Passovers in John's Gospel (also 6:4 and 11:55/19:14), giving the ministry a three-year framework. Anebē eis Ierosolyma — went up to Jerusalem: the verb anabainō is used for pilgrimage precisely because Jerusalem is geographically elevated (at ~750 m) and theologically the place of ascent.
14: ἐν τῷ ἱερῷ τοὺς πωλοῦντας βόας καὶ πρόβατα καὶ περιστεράς — The Evangelist uses hieron (G2411, the entire temple precincts including the Court of the Gentiles) not naos (G3485, the inner sanctuary). The commerce was in the outer courts, theologically the space designated for Gentile worship. The money changers (kollybistai) exchanged foreign currency for the Tyrian shekel required for temple tax — a necessary function, but one that had moved from the Mount of Olives into the sacred courts.
15: φραγέλλιον ἐκ σχοινίων — Phragellion (G5416) is a Latinism — from flagellum — indicating the Evangelist's familiarity with Roman terminology. The scourge was made from cords (scholniōn), likely the rope used to tether animals. Execheen (poured out) the money — the coins were scattered; anetrepsen (overturned) the tables; exebalen (drove out) the traders. Three distinct actions, three different verbs: the act is described with eyewitness specificity.
16: μὴ ποιεῖτε τὸν οἶκον τοῦ πατρός μου οἶκον ἐμπορίου — The contrast is between oikos tou patros mou (house of my Father — temple as divine dwelling) and oikos emporiou (market house — temple as commercial venue). The possessive patros mou (my Father's) is a Christological claim: Jesus speaks of the temple as his Father's house with a filial authority that positions him above even the legitimate temple authorities.
17: ὁ ζῆλος τοῦ οἴκου σου καταφάγεταί με — The Evangelist notes that the disciples remembered Ps 69:9 — but with a significant difference: the LXX of Ps 69:9 reads katephagen (aorist — consumed), whereas the disciples' citation uses the future kataphagetai (will consume). The future tense projects the cost of Jesus's temple zeal forward — anticipating what his action will ultimately cost him. Zēlos (G2205, zeal) connotes passionate, consuming devotion.
18: τί σημεῖον δεικνύεις ἡμῖν — The authorities respond to a prophetic action by demanding a legitimating sign. Sēmeion again: the request for a credential-miracle to authorize an extraordinary claim. The irony, developed across the Gospel, is that Jesus has been performing signs, and they will continue to demand signs while rejecting them. Exousian (authority, G1849) is the underlying issue: by what right does he act?
19: λύσατε τὸν ναὸν τοῦτον — The verb is aorist imperative: lysate (destroy!). This is not Jesus commanding the temple's destruction but framing a conditional: "If you destroy this temple, I will raise it in three days." The word is naos (G3485) — the inner sanctuary, the most sacred space — not the broader hieron of v.14. Jesus deliberately raises the stakes. Egerō (I will raise, future active) has Jesus as the agent of resurrection — a claim his opponents will not grasp and the disciples will only understand retrospectively (v.22).
20: τεσσεράκοντα καὶ ἕξ ἔτεσιν οἰκοδομήθη ὁ ναὸς οὗτος — The 46-year figure is historically useful: Herod's temple renovation began ca. 20/19 BC (Josephus, Ant. 15.380). Forty-six years later places this exchange approximately AD 26–27, consistent with the chronology deduced from Luke 3:1. The authorities' response demonstrates literal misunderstanding — they hear "this temple" as the Herodian building, not a body. Sy egerei — you (emphatic) will raise it?
21: ἐκεῖνος δὲ ἔλεγεν περὶ τοῦ ναοῦ τοῦ σώματος αὐτοῦ — The Evangelist inserts his editorial clarification: ekeinos de elegen (but that one was speaking) — a retrospective explanation impossible to the original hearers, possible only from the post-resurrection vantage point. Sōma (G4983) is the word the Evangelist chooses for the body — not sarx (flesh) or anthrōpos — marking it as the physical body that will die and rise.
22: ὅτε οὖν ἠγέρθη ἐκ νεκρῶν ἐμνήσθησαν — The resurrection (ēgerthē ek nekrōn) is the interpretive key the disciples lacked during the ministry. Emnēsthēsan (they remembered, aorist): a specific, time-bounded act of retrospective understanding. Episteusan tē graphē kai tō logō hon eipen ho Iēsous — they believed the scripture and the word Jesus had spoken: two objects of faith, both confirmed by the resurrection event.
23: ἐπίστευσαν εἰς τὸ ὄνομα αὐτοῦ θεωροῦντες αὐτοῦ τὰ σημεῖα — The crowd in Jerusalem believes (episteusan eis to onoma — the same formulation as 1:12) on the basis of witnessing signs. The following verses will distinguish this sign-based crowd-faith from the deeper trust that Jesus does not reciprocate.
24: αὐτὸς δὲ Ἰησοῦς οὐκ ἐπίστευεν ἑαυτὸν αὐτοῖς — A precise wordplay: the crowd believed in him (episteusan, v.23) but he did not entrust himself (episteuein heauton) to them. The same verb (pisteuō) applies to both: their faith in him and his trust of himself to them. Dia to auton ginōskein pantas — because he himself knew all people: the present infinitive ginōskein marks his ongoing knowledge of human nature as the ground for his caution.
25: οὐ χρείαν εἶχεν ἵνα τις μαρτυρήσῃ περὶ τοῦ ἀνθρώπου — He had no need of anyone to testify to him about humanity: martyria (testimony) here reverses its usual direction — instead of human witnesses testifying about Jesus, he has no need of human witnesses testifying about humans to him. Autos gar eginōsken ti ēn en tō anthrōpō — for he himself knew what was in the person. The imperfect eginōsken (was knowing, ongoing) marks a characteristic and continuous divine insight into human interiority.
